## Fintech Laws and Regulations 2023

By [Kevin Tran](https://www.nelsonmullins.com/professionals/kevin-tran)

*Global Legal Insights (GLI)*

Richard B. Levin and Kevin Tran recently authored the chapter titled, "A Disturbance in the Force – Ripples in the Crypto Universe" published by Global Legal Insights (GLI). The chapter in GLI - FinTech 2023 focuses on the regulation of digital assets in the United States, including: (i) the regulation of digital assets that are deemed securities; (ii) the regulation of trading platforms that facilitate the trading of digital assets that are deemed securities; and (iii) the regulation of platforms that facilitate the clearance and settlement of digital assets that are deemed securities. The chapter concludes with a discussion of the focus of the President of the United States on the regulation and development of FinTech.

Levin is chair of the FinTech and Regulation Practice at Nelson Mullins Riley & Scarborough, LLP, and was one of the first lawyers to focus on the regulation of blockchain and digital assets. He is considered a thought leader in the FinTech space. Levin has been advising FinTech clients on legal and regulatory issues since the start of electronic trading in the late 1990s. His practice focuses on the representation of early stage and publicly traded companies in the FinTech space, including investment banks, broker-dealers, investment advisers, digital currency trading platforms, ATSs, exchanges, and custodians. Levin has been recognized by Chambers and Partners for his knowledge of securities regulatory matters, great relationships with regulators, for helping clients push the boundaries of the FinTech sector, and for his advice on matters such as broker-dealer licensing and alternative trading systems.

Tran assists financial institutions and technology clients with financial regulatory, securities, and corporate matters. He focuses on the representation of early stage, mid-stage, and publicly traded FinTech companies focused on blockchain technology, digital assets, and cryptocurrencies, including broker-dealers, digital asset and currency trading platforms, digital asset custodians, alternative trading systems, and securities exchanges and swap execution facilities.

Readers can access the 2023 journal for a fee [here](https://www.globallegalinsights.com/practice-areas/fintech-laws-and-regulations).

--- SOURCE 2: Fintech in 2023: What to Expect from the Regulatory Landscape ---
URL: https://www.ftassociation.org/fintech-in-2023-what-to-expect-from-the-regulatory-landscape/

SUMMARY:
Leading global law firm Morgan Lewis recently brought on Kristin Lee, former senior vice president and assistant general counsel in Citi’s bank regulatory group, as a partner, enhancing the firm’s deep experience in financial regulation. We also work in close collaboration with our fintech, technology, and financial services industry teams, where we are able to keep abreast of regulatory developments not only at the state and federal levels but in key global jurisdictions. The recent Treasury Department report finds that bank-fintech partnerships can be positive for consumers but highlights the need for regulators to provide a clear and consistently applied regulatory framework for those partnerships.

FULL CONTENT:
![Financial Technology Association](https://www.ftassociation.org/wp-content/uploads/2021/02/fta_header-logo-regular.png)
![Financial Technology Association](https://www.ftassociation.org/wp-content/uploads/2021/02/fta_header-style-mobile-logo-regular.png)

# Fintech in 2023: What to Expect from the Regulatory Landscape

![EOY Recap](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns%3D'http%3A%2F%2Fwww.w3.org%2F2000%2Fsvg'%20viewBox%3D'0%200%201600%20800'%2F%3E)

Leading global law firm Morgan Lewis recently brought on [Kristin Lee](https://email.ftassociation.org/e3t/Ctc/ZV+113/d2sNKB04/VVWrZq6yzf6hW34WDxD2-z4lFW3Z50n14VDzx2N3Qcf8k3lLB3V1-WJV7CgCQ_W1xYtPQ3Fc1bvW7DpJp_8Vq9nmW8kfhGy3ZbSJXW85LPYP60jHBqVDgPRb76LTsMW7Wvh2Y29L7YmW13K3hK3k581XW6VHv4-4m4LQ1W20qQ2m4zWL-QN4scYJ2w-Cs-N7hNVpMtqn4WW7Vxb5N7Dn-WcN6Pq0C2skntjW8KxK5x50rFn1W85b6VG8SbhN8V-7FTZ3-y7bcW53m6DY873qCLW7W5VMz6y8n4XW1F4m5865Jp-FW6BLT748rtjWG3j-y1), former senior vice president and assistant general counsel in Citi’s bank regulatory group, as a partner, enhancing the firm’s deep experience in financial regulation. In this edition of *Fintech Explained*, Kristin analyzes the state of play on upcoming fintech policy issues, including bank-fintech partnerships, and previews what’s ahead in 2023.

**Tell us about Morgan Lewis’ bank regulatory practice and how you are preparing to advise clients going into 2023.**

Morgan Lewis has a 150-member global investment management team that counsels most of the largest financial institutions on all key issues affecting them, with a particular focus on how their businesses operate and are regulated and how this affects their day-to-day dealings with clients and the financial markets. With a growing demand from clients and an evolving regulatory environment, the firm has made a strong investment in building out its bank regulatory capabilities.

Policymakers are keeping a watchful eye on banks and financial institutions, particularly during a time of economic uncertainty and with new players and technology continuing to enter the market. Drawing on a combination of in-house, public, and private sector experience, our team advises clients on key regulatory and enforcement developments coming from US banking regulators, as well as the innovation and development of new products and services. We also work in close collaboration with our fintech, technology, and financial services industry teams, where we are able to keep abreast of regulatory developments not only at the state and federal levels but in key global jurisdictions.

**What is on your radar from a regulatory and legislative perspective in 2023?**

As a result of the turbulence over the last year, there is a heightened focus on whether specific regulation with respect to digital assets is necessary. While some feel as though this space is largely underregulated and the industry would greatly benefit from a comprehensive US regulatory framework, others believe that existing laws and regulations are adequate. We continue to see bipartisan policy work by legislators, but none have gained sufficient traction to be adopted in the near term.

The federal banking agencies continue to take a careful and cautious approach to digital asset initiatives, recently highlighting crypto-asset risks to banking organizations. We will likely see continued enforcement in this space by various US financial regulators, as each regulator’s jurisdiction over the crypto industry remains uncertain.

We also expect further developments with respect to Federal Reserve master account access. We’ve seen, over the past couple of years, fintechs and other financial technology firms calling for broader access to obtaining master accounts and services with the Federal Reserve. Last summer, the Federal Reserve adopted final guidelines establishing a three-tiered approach to assess master account applicants. Then, in November, in an effort to bring greater transparency, the Fed proposed to periodically publish a list of institutions that have master accounts. After the Fed issued this proposal, Congress adopted a similar requirement, in Section 5708 of the National Defense Authorization Act for Fiscal Year 2023, that the Federal Reserve establish and maintain a public database that shows both which institutions have and have requested master account access. These developments have an eye toward the digital transformation of banking while staying grounded with principles of safety and soundness, and prudential regulation.

The 2023 US regulatory landscape will be exciting to watch!

**Bank-fintech partnerships can play a critical role in delivering value to consumers. Recently, federal banking agencies have bolstered oversight of third-party fintech partnerships. What are regulators’ primary concerns?**

The recent Treasury Department report finds that bank-fintech partnerships can be positive for consumers but highlights the need for regulators to provide a clear and consistently applied regulatory framework for those partnerships. The regulators are aware that the banking industry continues to become more complex and technologically driven, driving banks to form more numerous and complex relationships with fintech companies to remain competitive, expand operations, and help meet customer needs. The banking agencies are primarily focused on safety and soundness considerations but are also focused on, among other things, overall risk management practices, Bank Secrecy Act (BSA)/anti-money laundering risk management, suspicious activity reporting, and information technology control and risk governance.

To keep up with the latest regulatory developments impacting the financial services industry, visit Morgan Lewis’ [*All Things FinReg*](https://email.ftassociation.org/e3t/Ctc/ZV+113/d2sNKB04/VVWrZq6yzf6hW34WDxD2-z4lFW3Z50n14VDzx2N3Qcf8k3lLB3V1-WJV7CgFCLW4S_zs6945v9fW2tr7HN3MJSVNW8Dsvbw3yyjfWW88GhjN8WsFpQW3Wgjc-5sD46WW4rKrn2822YNzW7rt5_Y8qqggdW87bWFs15gtMfW7hhTpH4bXWH6W8rZ00p6xsnp-W4Z5PhH4-4jwzW2LdHY736KbG0W3DTB9V8HJzjcW5p5Q-P9j1ygxW5rydqM2gFsSMW4ywKJn8SZBvrW7KZ7nc8FxLPFW8SFXCQ1CjkgYN79712yx9Y4CW6YSt9Z3H5TV12kX1) blog. [Subscribe here](https://email.ftassociation.org/e3t/Ctc/ZV+113/d2sNKB04/VVWrZq6yzf6hW34WDxD2-z4lFW3Z50n14VDzx2N3Qcf9Q3lLCyV1-WJV7CgR4MVPvqc_3CV0lYW8WrZ_P6bVwQFW4NZYt91HRLgyW30j0bM2lWnt3W9hB7612lf-GPVzMN3z8Tt_LwW6GrpNP6XtW7tW30Tl8w3KMz0VW2kDrT794ly_sV9Yxtt7WQHy0W1F_znw28tYKDW78JS0880s9zXW1MkXTt7QVbX6W9m3XVd7LGFJBW2yqqNP63G5HyW1P0F7G7Ch__fW4r4lYg8WJdZmW1-MhKW23RkNgW1kXXc15mQzkrW5vyYC39cPHr8W8zL89T14tjpwW1rwwYW7QqJSwW98vbDG1_86M6N9dscq57trGHW8M6l_94rS9cZW3fdxVM8tnDN8W34gjvj1f_knHN1NWNlDNZfBFW4y3dzj6_CF97W975_Ym6Wd5nT36SM1).

Fintech’s Next Big Challenge? Thriving In An Era Of Regulatory Uncertainty

By[Nandan Sheth](https://www.forbes.com/sites/nandansheth/ "https://www.forbes.com/sites/nandansheth/")

[Money](https://www.forbes.com/money/)

[Fintech](https://www.forbes.com/fintech/)

# Fintech’s Next Big Challenge? Thriving In An Era Of Regulatory Uncertainty

By[Nandan Sheth](https://www.forbes.com/sites/nandansheth/ "https://www.forbes.com/sites/nandansheth/"),

Contributor.

Forbes contributors publish independent expert analyses and insights.

 I'm CEO of the fintech startup Splitit, plus an angel investor.

Mar 05, 2025, 02:00pm EST

The financial services industry is on the cusp of a profound regulatory transformation. With shifting political and economic landscapes, regulatory bodies across the globe are rethinking their approach to financial oversight, bringing both challenges and opportunities for fintechs and traditional institutions alike. In the U.S., the Consumer Financial Protection Bureau (CFPB), the Office of the Comptroller of the Currency (OCC), and the Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation (FDIC) are evolving in response to market dynamics. Meanwhile, international markets are grappling with emerging frameworks that could redefine fintech’s competitive edge.

At the same time, cryptocurrency is undergoing a fundamental transformation, shifting from a speculative asset to a regulated financial instrument. The rise of central bank digital currencies (CBDCs), increasing institutional adoption, and heightened regulatory scrutiny are reshaping the way digital assets fit into the broader financial ecosystem. Governments worldwide are recognizing the need for oversight to protect consumers and stabilize financial systems, while financial institutions are exploring blockchain technology and tokenized assets as part of their long-term strategies. The next five years will determine how this landscape unfolds, with a delicate balance between fostering innovation and imposing regulation that could either accelerate or stifle progress.

As financial institutions navigate these changes, here are three key takeaways that fintechs should keep in mind. The most successful players will be those that embrace uncertainty, adapt with agility, and view regulatory evolution as an opportunity for innovation rather than an obstacle.

PROMOTED

**1. Embracing Uncertainty: Navigating the New Regulatory Landscape in Financial Services**

In the U.S., regulatory shifts have historically been a defining force in financial services. The CFPB, for instance, issued over $3.5 billion in fines in 2023 alone for consumer protection violations, reinforcing the importance of compliance in fintech’s growth strategies. However, potential regulatory rollbacks could create a more business-friendly environment, particularly in fintech lending, embedded finance, and digital banking. A Deloitte survey found that over 60% of fintechs view regulatory uncertainty as their top challenge, emphasizing the urgency of adaptive strategies.

Many legacy financial institutions are becoming more receptive to innovative approaches and the transformative potential of emerging technology. This shift presents a prime opportunity for forward-thinking fintechs to develop lean technology frameworks, laying the groundwork for future partnerships. However, fintechs that fail to take early action risk being blindsided and left behind when regulatory conditions finally enable these institutions to invest in emerging tech.

On a global scale, regulatory frameworks are evolving in ways that could either enhance or hinder fintech growth. The European Union’s PSD3 and the UK’s Consumer Duty regulations are pushing for stronger consumer protections, while markets like India and Brazil are tightening compliance requirements for digital lending. The Buy Now, Pay Later (BNPL) sector—once an area of rapid expansion—now faces heightened scrutiny, with markets like Australia mandating formal credit checks for providers, aligning them more closely with traditional lenders. Yet, history has shown that regulatory clarity can drive innovation, as evidenced by the UK’s open banking framework, which led to a 300% increase in API-driven financial services.

This shift extends beyond traditional financial services. The $156 trillion cross-border payments market is at an inflection point, with inefficiencies in legacy financial infrastructure creating new opportunities for disruption. Fintech innovators are stepping in to streamline transactions, while crypto-backed solutions like stablecoins are offering real-time settlements and reduced transaction fees. Whether governments will embrace these innovations or double down on protecting legacy financial institutions remains an open question, but the stakes are clear: as digital commerce, supply chains, and remote work accelerate demand for efficient global transactions, the winners will be those who anticipate and adapt to regulatory shifts.

**2. Agility in Action: How Financial Institutions Can Thrive Amid Regulatory Shifts**

A look to the past reveals that regulatory change has often acted as a catalyst for financial innovation. The Dodd-Frank Act of 2010 paved the way for digital-first banks and alternative lenders, GDPR in 2018 reshaped fintech’s approach to data privacy and transparency, and the Durbin Amendment of 2011 led to new fintech-bank collaborations. More recently, traditional banks and fintechs have increased partnerships by 30% year over year, as banks seek fintech agility while maintaining regulatory stability.

With cryptocurrency now moving into a more structured regulatory framework, major financial institutions are positioning themselves for deeper integration of blockchain-based solutions. The industry’s challenge is to balance compliance with the need for innovation—whether that means leveraging RegTech solutions to streamline oversight or aligning business models with evolving rules on digital assets and payments. The RegTech market is projected to reach $19.5 billion by 2027, signaling a growing reliance on AI-driven compliance automation. Additionally, 85% of financial institutions plan to invest in compliance technology over the next two years, underscoring the importance of proactive regulatory engagement.

**3. From Roadblock to Runway: Leveraging Regulatory Changes for Financial Innovation**

Fintechs that treat regulatory shifts as an opportunity rather than a constraint will be the ones that succeed in this new era. The BNPL sector, for instance, can future-proof itself by evolving hybrid credit models that align with stricter regulations. Likewise, fintechs investing in modular technology stacks that allow for swift compliance updates will be better positioned to scale as rules continue to evolve.

Strategic partnerships will be critical in navigating these shifts. Banks, fintechs, and RegTech firms must collaborate with policymakers to create regulations that encourage innovation while protecting consumers. Already, 40% of traditional financial institutions are exploring BNPL and embedded lending partnerships, recognizing the value of aligning with fintech disruptors. As these collaborations accelerate, the financial ecosystems will undergo a profound transformation. The new-age fintechs that emerge as leaders will flatten the ecosystem—reshaping the industry as we know it. Over the next decade, payments and financial services may look entirely different, driven by embedded finance, AI, and seamless digital experiences that put the customer first.

The financial services industry has always been shaped by regulatory cycles. While uncertainty is inevitable, the firms that focus on what they can control—building adaptable business models, leveraging technology for compliance, and fostering strategic alliances—will emerge as leaders. Those who see compliance not as a burden but as a driver of competitive advantage will be the ones shaping the next chapter of fintech innovation.

enriched_points.expanded_content: FinTechs face complex regulations across jurisdictions, requiring compliance with laws governing digital assets, data protection, and anti-money laundering, often resulting in operational delays and increased costs.
The FinTech landscape is saturated with startups and traditional banks vying for market share, leading to aggressive pricing strategies and innovation races that challenge sustainable growth.

Economic instability, inflation, and interest rate fluctuations significantly impact consumer behavior and investment in FinTech, necessitating adaptive strategies to mitigate risks and seize opportunities.
FinTech companies must overcome challenges related to legacy systems, integration complexities, and cybersecurity threats, requiring ongoing investment in technology to remain competitive and secure.
